questions.question: Explain the difference between the SIMD and MIMD architectural classifications in Flynn's Taxonomy. Provide a real-world hardware example for each. (3 marks)
keyPoints: SIMD: Single Instruction operates on Multiple Data elements simultaneously. Example: GPU, AVX vector instructions.
MIMD: Multiple Instructions operate on Multiple Data streams concurrently and independently. Example: multi-core CPU, computer cluster.
Key contrast: SIMD has centralized control (one instruction broadcast to all), MIMD has decentralized control (each processor independent).

questions.question: State Amdahl's Law and explain its significance for parallel computing. If 20% of a program is sequential, what is the maximum speedup achievable? (3 marks)
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1 / (s + (1-s)/p), where s = serial fraction, p = number of processors.
Significance: It shows that the serial fraction fundamentally limits speedup, regardless of the number of processors.
With 20% serial (s = 0.2): maximum speedup (infinite processors) = 1/0.2 = 5x. Even with a million processors, you cannot exceed 5x speedup.

questions.question: Compare and contrast shared-memory and distributed-memory parallel architectures. Include their key characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and programming models. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Shared memory: single global address space, all processors can access any memory location.
Distributed memory: private per-processor memory, no global address space.
Advantage of shared memory: easier programming (OpenMP, Pthreads), implicit data sharing.
Advantage of distributed memory: better scalability, no cache coherence overhead, cost-effective commodity hardware.
Disadvantage of shared memory: limited scalability (bus bottleneck, cache coherence), synchronization complexity.
Disadvantage of distributed memory: harder programming (MPI), explicit communication, communication latency.
Hybrid systems combine both: MPI between nodes + OpenMP within nodes.

questions.question: Why did the computer industry shift from increasing single-core clock speeds to multi-core architectures? Discuss the implications for software development. (5 marks)
keyPoints: The 'power wall': increasing clock speed exponentially increased power consumption and heat, hitting physical limits around 2005.
The 'memory wall': memory access speeds did not keep pace with processor speeds, limiting single-core performance gains.
The 'ILP wall': diminishing returns from extracting more instruction-level parallelism from sequential code.
Multi-core provides more performance within the same power budget by running multiple cores at lower frequencies.

Implication for software: programs must be explicitly parallelized to benefit from multi-core. Sequential programs cannot automatically use additional cores. This shifted the burden from hardware to software developers.
